claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You do not live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 13 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 13 (under 16)
  - Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant does not live in the UK; Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
circumstances: Claimant does not live in the UK
Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine your eligibility for Child Benefit. To do this, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances and the children you're responsible for.

Let me start with the first question:

**Do you currently live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I don't live in the UK at the moment. I'm living abroad, but I wanted to check if I could still claim Child Benefit for my child.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. It's good that you've checked, as there are some circumstances where you may still be able to claim Child Benefit while living abroad.

Before I can advise you further, I need to understand your situation better.

**What is your nationality, or do you have any specific immigration status (for example, are you a UK citizen, or do you have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I'm a UK citizen. I'm just living abroad temporarily at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that you're a UK citizen living abroad temporarily. That's helpful information.

Since you're a UK citizen living abroad, there are specific rules that may apply to you. I need to ask:

**Which country are you currently living in?**

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Now I have a better picture of your situation.

Before I can fully assess your eligibility, I need to know more about the child you mentioned:

**What is your child's name and how old are they?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: My child's name is Alex and they're 13 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information about Alex.

Now I need to understand your relationship to Alex and your financial contribution:

**Are you living with Alex in Spain, or is Alex living with someone else (such as remaining in the UK with another parent or family member)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is living with me here in Spain.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming that Alex is living with you in Spain.

I have one more important question to clarify your eligibility:

**Are you a Crown servant (such as someone working for the UK government, civil service, or diplomatic service) posted abroad?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I'm not a Crown servant. I'm not working for the UK government or anything like that.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that clarification. I now have all the information I need to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit.

Based on the information you've provided, I need to explain the rules that apply to your situation:

According to UK Child Benefit rules, you may be able to get Child Benefit if you go to live in certain countries **or** if you're a Crown servant. The guidance specifically states that living in certain countries allows for eligibility, and Crown servants also qualify.

However, the detailed rules about which specific countries qualify for Child Benefit eligibility when you're living abroad are contained in the "living abroad" section of the guidance. As a UK citizen living in Spain who is not a Crown servant, your eligibility would depend on whether Spain is one of the countries where Child Benefit can be claimed.

The guidance I have access to indicates that there are specific countries where you can claim Child Benefit while living abroad, but it doesn't provide the complete list of those countries in the information available to me. 

**To get a definitive answer about your eligibility, you would need to contact the Child Benefit Office directly**, as they can confirm whether Spain is included in the countries where you can claim, and they can provide you with all the specific details about your situation.

I apologize that I cannot give you a more definitive answer based on the information I have available. Is there anything else I can clarify about the general Child Benefit rules in the meantime?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
I see, so I need to contact the Child Benefit Office directly to find out if Spain qualifies. That's helpful to know. 

Just to clarify though - even if Spain was on the list of qualifying countries, is there anything else about my situation that would affect my eligibility? For instance, does it matter that Alex's other parent is also in Spain with us and they already claim Child Benefit for Alex?

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Ah, that's a very important clarification, and it actually does affect your eligibility significantly.

According to the Child Benefit rules, **only one person can get Child Benefit for a child**. Since Alex's other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for Alex, you would not be able to claim as well.

The guidance is clear on this point: "Two people cannot get Child Benefit for the same child. If you and someone else are responsible for the same child, agree between yourselves who will get Child Benefit."

So even if Spain is a qualifying country for living abroad, because Alex's other parent is already receiving Child Benefit for Alex, **you would not be eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex**.

If you believe you should be the one claiming instead (for any reason), you and the other parent would need to agree to switch who receives the payments. If you cannot agree, HMRC would make the decision for you. But you cannot both claim for the same child.

I hope that clarifies the situation for you. Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, that makes sense. So because Alex's other parent is already claiming it, I can't claim it as well. I understand now. Thank you for explaining that to me.
